ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste; closest parent is spice paste (food product) FOODON_03301710.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Sauces and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific Mee Siam paste code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste or an equivalent spice paste product.

serving.description: 1 serving (half a packet / approximately 50 g paste used per dish)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste is approximately 50 g (half a standard 100 g retail packet), used to prepare one portion of Mee Siam noodles for one person.

nutrient_source.title: Spice paste / sambal-type condiment — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference entries for chilli-based spice pastes and belacan-containing condiments, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian spice pastes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by compositional analogy with chilli-based sambal / spice paste products (USDA FDC NDB No. 02009 and related entries) and typical Mee Siam paste ingredient profiles documented in FAO/INFOODS SEA. Energy and macros reflect a moderately oil-containing, high-sodium, moderate-carbohydrate spice paste. Values should be treated as estimates pending a direct laboratory analysis or HPB-verified entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated by analogy with chilli-based spice pastes and tamarind-containing condiments; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy with mixed chilli-tamarind-sugar spice pastes, which contain moderate sugar content (approximately 10 g/100 g) balanced by fibre, protein, and fat that attenuate glycaemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database lists similar sambal and chilli paste products in the low-to-medium GI range (GI 45–58). GL per 50 g serving = (52 × 11.0 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1450 mg per 100 g; ~725 mg per 50 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should use Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste sparingly or seek a reduced-sodium formulation, as a single 50 g serving contributes approximately 725 mg sodium — roughly 32% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~10 g/100 g) and estimated low GI of 52
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can use this paste in controlled portions; the low estimated GI (52) and presence of dietary fibre and fat moderate the glycaemic response, but the sugar content (~5 g per 50 g serving) should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~725 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~1.25 g per serving) warrant portion control; using a smaller quantity of paste and pairing with low-sodium ingredients can help manage overall cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Siam Ready-to-Cook Paste is a pre-blended Singaporean spice paste designed to simplify the preparation of Mee Siam, a popular Singaporean and Malaysian dish of thin rice vermicelli served in a tangy, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet gravy. The paste typically contains dried shrimp, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, dried chilli, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), tamarind, sugar, salt, and vegetable oil — all ground and cooked together into a concentrated flavour base. When used, the paste is stir-fried briefly before adding water or coconut milk, tofu, beansprouts, and rice vermicelli to complete the dish. The flavour profile is complex: savoury and umami from the dried shrimp and belacan, sour from tamarind, mildly spicy from dried chilli, and subtly sweet from added sugar. The texture of the paste itself is thick and oily. Nutritionally, the paste is energy-dense (~220 kcal/100 g) due to its oil content, and is notably high in sodium (~1450 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It contains moderate carbohydrates (~22 g/100 g) including sugars (~10 g/100 g), with a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 52, making it relatively moderate in glycaemic impact when used in typical serving quantities. The paste is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to its shrimp and belacan content. It is generally considered halal. This product is sold at Singaporean food establishments such as Toast Box and is also available in retail form. It is a culturally significant convenience product that preserves the authentic flavour of traditional Mee Siam while reducing preparation time.
common_user_queries: What is Mee Siam paste made of?
How many calories in Mee Siam paste?
Is Mee Siam paste suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Mee Siam paste?
Can I use Mee Siam paste for a low-sodium diet?
What is the GI of Mee Siam paste?
How do I cook with Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste?
What does Mee Siam taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mee Siam ready-to-cook paste exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by compositional analogy with chilli-based sambal and spice paste products from approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient profile of this product class is well-documented and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from compositional analogy with chilli-based spice pastes and sambal-type condiments using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. A direct laboratory analysis or HPB-verified entry would upgrade confidence to high. 2. Sodium content is estimated at ~1450 mg/100 g based on typical belacan-containing paste formulations; actual sodium may vary by brand and formulation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation convention for vegetable-oil-based spice pastes without hydrogenated fats. 4. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the analogous source entries used for estimation and cannot be reliably inferred without direct analysis. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. The cuisine tag in the input JSON was listed as 'Toast' (reflecting the field survey restaurant category Toast Box); this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as the canonical cuisine classification for Mee Siam. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical commercial Mee Siam paste formulations sold in Singapore; individual product halal certification should be verified.
